ExtraaLearn Project¶

Context¶

The EdTech industry has been surging in the past decade immensely, and according to a forecast, the Online Education market would be worth $286.62bn by 2023 with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 10.26% from 2018 to 2023. The modern era of online education has enforced a lot in its growth and expansion beyond any limit. Due to having many dominant features like ease of information sharing, personalized learning experience, transparency of assessment, etc, it is now preferable to traditional education.

In the present scenario due to the Covid-19, the online education sector has witnessed rapid growth and is attracting a lot of new customers. Due to this rapid growth, many new companies have emerged in this industry. With the availability and ease of use of digital marketing resources, companies can reach out to a wider audience with their offerings. The customers who show interest in these offerings are termed as leads. There are various sources of obtaining leads for Edtech companies, like

  • The customer interacts with the marketing front on social media or other online platforms.
  • The customer browses the website/app and downloads the brochure
  • The customer connects through emails for more information.

The company then nurtures these leads and tries to convert them to paid customers. For this, the representative from the organization connects with the lead on call or through email to share further details.

Objective¶

ExtraaLearn is an initial stage startup that offers programs on cutting-edge technologies to students and professionals to help them upskill/reskill. With a large number of leads being generated on a regular basis, one of the issues faced by ExtraaLearn is to identify which of the leads are more likely to convert so that they can allocate resources accordingly. You, as a data scientist at ExtraaLearn, have been provided the leads data to:

  • Analyze and build an ML model to help identify which leads are more likely to convert to paid customers,
  • Find the factors driving the lead conversion process
  • Create a profile of the leads which are likely to convert

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of leads and their interaction details with ExtraaLearn. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary

  • ID: ID of the lead

  • age: Age of the lead

  • current_occupation: Current occupation of the lead. Values include 'Professional','Unemployed',and 'Student'

  • first_interaction: How did the lead first interacted with ExtraaLearn. Values include 'Website', 'Mobile App'

  • profile_completed: What percentage of profile has been filled by the lead on the website/mobile app. Values include Low - (0-50%), Medium - (50-75%), High (75-100%)

  • website_visits: How many times has a lead visited the website

  • time_spent_on_website: Total time spent on the website

  • page_views_per_visit: Average number of pages on the website viewed during the visits.

  • last_activity: Last interaction between the lead and ExtraaLearn.

    • Email Activity: Seeking for details about program through email, Representative shared information with lead like brochure of program , etc
    • Phone Activity: Had a Phone Conversation with representative, Had conversation over SMS with representative, etc
    • Website Activity: Interacted on live chat with representative, Updated profile on website, etc

  • print_media_type1: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn in the Newspaper.

  • print_media_type2: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn in the Magazine.

  • digital_media: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn on the digital platforms.

  • educational_channels: Flag indicating whether the lead had heard about ExtraaLearn in the education channels like online forums, discussion threads, educational websites, etc.

  • referral: Flag indicating whether the lead had heard about ExtraaLearn through reference.

  • status: Flag indicating whether the lead was converted to a paid customer or not.

Importing necessary libraries and data¶

In [85]:
# Importing libraries for data manipulation
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Importing libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# To ignore warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Removes the limit for the number of displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

# Sets the limit for the number of displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 200)

# To build models for prediction
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, BaggingClassifier, AdaBoostClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# To encode categorical variables
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

# For tuning the model
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV

# To check model performance
from sklearn.metrics import (
    make_scorer,
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
    roc_auc_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    classification_report,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
)
In [86]:
# Read the healthcare dataset file
original_data = pd.read_csv("/content/ExtraaLearn PotentialCustomerPred.csv")
In [87]:
# Copying data in case we need the original dataset
data = original_data.copy()

  • The number of non-null values is the same as the total number of entries in the data, there are no null values.
  • The column ID is an identifier for leads in the data. This column will not help with our analysis so we can drop it.

Observations:

  • On average, the lead age is 46 years old, mininum age is 18 and maximum is 63.
  • There are 3 visits to the website in average per lead, but there are leads that have not been in the website (min 0), and there are others that have visited the website 30 times!.

  • On average the pages views are 3, with a maximum of 18 page views per visit.
  • On average, the status of the leads have a min of 0 and a maximum of 1. The status column represent the unpaid vs the paid customers and it is our target variable.

Observations:

  • The majority of leads (~55%) are professionals, which shows there are lots of professionals looking to upskill or reskill.
  • The first interaction of 55% of the leads was through the website, which is not surprising since we are in a digital era and the people do not download or visit an application unless they are going to use it often.
  • The profile completion is acceptable in general 98%, 49% having high completion, ~49% medium level, and there are very few (2%) in low range whose have not completed their profile.
  • The last activity of (~50%) was by email, this is a point to analyze to understand if this represent the % that is already converted to a paid customer. The Website activity and phone activity is pretty similar, being around the 24% and 27% respectively.
  • The print_media_type1/newspaper sourced has a 10%, the print_media_type2/magazine sourced has 5%, the digital_media platforms as sourced of leads contribute with an 11%, the educational_channels with a 15%, and referral with just 2%. All of this percentages are in terms of the total quantity of leads per variable, treated as independent variables. So those variables such as print_media_type1, print_media_type2, digital_media and educational_channels need to be analysed together, to understand which is the percentage of leads that belong to each source taking in consideration the big picture/marketing leads. We do not know if they are mutually excluding the rest of them.

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Questions

  1. Leads will have different expectations from the outcome of the course and the current occupation may play a key role in getting them to participate in the program. Find out how current occupation affects lead status.
  2. The company's first impression on the customer must have an impact. Do the first channels of interaction have an impact on the lead status?
  3. The company uses multiple modes to interact with prospects. Which way of interaction works best?
  4. The company gets leads from various channels such as print media, digital media, referrals, etc. Which of these channels have the highest lead conversion rate?
  5. People browsing the website or mobile application are generally required to create a profile by sharing their personal data before they can access additional information.Does having more details about a prospect increase the chances of conversion?

Univariate Analysis¶

Numerical Variables

In [101]:
#Histogram for numerical columns
hist_axes = data[num_cols].hist(figsize = (14, 14))
for row_axes in hist_axes:
    for ax in row_axes:
        for patch in ax.patches:
            patch.set_facecolor('violet')
plt.show()

Observations:

  • The age distribution is skewed to the left, with a peak around 50 years old, but there is still a good portion of leads spread between the range of the 20s to the 50s.
  • Website_visits and Pages_viewed_per_visit are right-skewed distribution, meaning most of the leads visit the website few times and do not visit lots of pages in their visits.
  • Time_spent_on_website is like a bimodal distribution, where some leads are around 240 seconds and other group of leads is around 2000 seconds, maybe this is the difference between the unpaid and paid customers.
  • Status is a Binomial distribution, indicating that the majority of the leads, more than 3,000, were not converted to paid customers, and just around 1,350 got converted.
In [102]:
# Function to plot a boxplot to visualize outliers and a histogram along the same scale

def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(12, 7), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (12,7))
    kde: whether to the show density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows = 2,      # Number of rows of the subplot grid = 2
        sharex = True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw = {"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize = figsize,
    )                   # Creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(data = data, x = feature, ax = ax_box2, showmeans = True, color = "violet"
    )                   # Boxplot will be created and a star will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data = data, x = feature, kde = kde, ax = ax_hist2, bins = bins, palette = "#ccebc5"
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data = data, x = feature, kde = kde, ax = ax_hist2
    )                   # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color = "green", linestyle = "--"
    )                   # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color = "black", linestyle = "-"
    )                   # Add median to the histogram
In [103]:
#To visualize current Lead Status distribution
histogram_boxplot(data, "status", kde = True, bins = 30)
In [104]:
#To check how many leads have not converted to paid customers
data['status'].value_counts()
Out[104]:
count
status
0 3223
1 1375

Observations:

  • This is a binary distribution where the big majority of the leads, more than 3000 in fact, are not converted to paid customers, and just around 1375 leads were converted.
  • There is no other option here, a lead will convert or not convert, which translate a classification problem.
In [105]:
# Visualize Age
histogram_boxplot(data, "age", kde = True, bins = 30)

Skewed to the left. Lots of the leads are above their 50s. The mean is around 45 years old, but there are younger people, all adults from 18 years to up, interested in Edtech online education.

In [106]:
# Visualize the Website visits
histogram_boxplot(data, "website_visits", kde = True, bins = 30)

Skewed to the right. The majority of the leads have visited the website less than 10 times and the peak of the distribution shows website visits between 3 to 4 times in average. There are some outliers that have visited the site more that 10 times.

In [107]:
# Visualize the Time Spend on the website
histogram_boxplot(data, "time_spent_on_website", kde = True, bins = 30)

Interesting distribution where there are 2 clear areas very distance. The distribution shows leads that spent up to ~2500 seconds/42 minutes, but in average the time in the website per lead is ~750 seconds/12 minutes. I need to analyze this variable in conjuction with another one to better understand this distribution behaviour.

In [108]:
# Vizualize pages view per visit
histogram_boxplot(data, "page_views_per_visit", kde = True, bins = 30)

Long tail to the right shows lots of outliers of the variable page_views_per_visit that go aproximately from 6 up to 18. Mean is around 3 pages viewed per visit, but there is a peak in the curve that shows around 1400 leads that have just visited ~2 pages.

Categorical Variables

Below we are creating subplots to plot the categorical variables in a single frame.

In [109]:
# To create a block of subplots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 2, figsize = (18, 18))

fig.suptitle('Bar plot for all categorical variables in the dataset')

sns.countplot(ax = axes[0, 0], x = 'current_occupation', data = data, color = '#ccebc5',
              order = data['current_occupation'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[0, 1], x = 'first_interaction', data = data, color = 'violet',
              order = data['first_interaction'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[1, 0], x = 'profile_completed', data = data, color = '#ccebc5',
              order = data['profile_completed'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[1, 1], x = 'last_activity', data = data, color = 'violet',
              order = data['last_activity'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[2, 0], x = 'print_media_type1', data = data, color = '#ccebc5',
              order = data['print_media_type1'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[2, 1], x = 'print_media_type2', data = data, color = 'violet',
              order = data['print_media_type2'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[3, 0], x = 'digital_media', data = data, color = '#ccebc5',
              order = data['print_media_type1'].value_counts().index);

sns.countplot(ax = axes[3, 1], x = 'educational_channels', data = data, color = 'violet',
              order = data['print_media_type2'].value_counts().index);
In [110]:
# Graph for referral because it could not be included in the previous set
plt.figure(figsize = (4,3))
sns.countplot(x = 'referral', data = data, color ='violet')
Out[110]:
<Axes: xlabel='referral', ylabel='count'>

Observations in reference to the subplots and the previous data count and proportions calculated for categorical feautures:

  • Most of the leads are professionals ~2,500 (56%).
  • The first interaction by website ~2,500 (55%) is slighly higher than the mobile app interaction ~2,000 (45%).
  • The profile completion is between and high and medium. There is a very small portion of profiles with low completion in the data.
  • The last activity is higher by Email (49%) than it is by phone (27%) or website activity (24%). The email way of interaction is more common since more than 2000 leads prefer it over the phone interaction which has ~1250 and email with ~1000 leads.
  • The origin of the leads Printed_media_type 1, printed_media_type2, digital_media, educational_channels and referral have very low chances of convertion to paid customers
  • The referral convertion rate is very very low around 2% with only 93 leads converted out of the total of leads 4,598.

Bivariate Analysis¶

We have analyzed different categorical and numerical variables alone. Let's now check how does STATUS is related with other numerical and categorical variables

Relationship between STATUS and Numerical variables

In [111]:
# The mean of numerical variables grouped by STATUS
data.groupby(['status'])[num_cols].mean()
Out[111]:
age website_visits time_spent_on_website page_views_per_visit status
status
0 45.125349 3.592305 579.570586 3.036998 0.0
1 48.649455 3.543273 1069.952000 3.031453 1.0

Observations:

  • There is no real difference between the leads with average age converted to paid customer ~45 years old, and the leads that did not convert with average age ~48yo.

  • Similar situation happens between the website_visits and page_views_per_visit variables, it seems that the outliers are not affecting that much. Both variables shows very similar quantity of visits in average ~3.5, and almost the same page_views_per_visit ~3.03.

  • The only variable that makes the difference here is the time_spent_on_website. Leads converted to paid customers spent almost double the time in the website than the leads that did not convert to paid customers. It seems that the time invested is related with the deep interest of the customers and its further enrollment.

Relationship between Numerical variables

In [112]:
# Plotting the correlation between numerical variables
plt.figure(figsize = (13, 8))

sns.heatmap(data[num_cols].corr(), annot = True, fmt = '0.2f', cmap = 'YlGn')
Out[112]:
<Axes: >

Observations:

  • Confirming the previous observation, the Time spent of website and status are the only variables are slighly correlated with each other, and all of the rest numerical variables are not really correlated with the resulting status of the leads.
  • The numerical variables like age show little or no correlation with the target variable (Status), which would indicate that the categorical variables might be more important for the prediction.

Other numerical functions

In [113]:
# function to plot distributions wrt target

def distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, predictor, target):

    fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))
    target_uniq = data[target].unique()
    axs[0, 0].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[0]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[0]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 0],
        color="violet",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[0, 1].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[1]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[1]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 1],
        color="#ccebc5",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[1, 0].set_title("Boxplot w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=target, y=predictor, ax=axs[1, 0], palette=["violet", "#ccebc5"])

    axs[1, 1].set_title("Boxplot (without outliers) w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data,
        x=target,
        y=predictor,
        ax=axs[1, 1],
        showfliers=False,
        palette= ["violet", "#ccebc5"],
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
In [114]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "website_visits", "status")

The number of website_visits is very similar in both status even when there is outliers presence. We already saw a similar conclussion in the table of mean of the numerical variables vs status

In [115]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "time_spent_on_website", "status")

This graph is very interesting, it shows that the paid customers come from higher times spent on the website.

In [116]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "age", "status")

Clearly above than 40 years old the leads are more willing to enroll than younger leads, the mean in fact is approximately 53 years old. The age variable can be improved to focus in converting younger populations as well.

Relationship between STATUS and Categorical variables

In [117]:
for i in cat_cols:
    if i != 'status':
        (pd.crosstab(data[i], data['status'], normalize = 'index')*100).plot(kind = 'bar', figsize = (6, 3), stacked = True,
        color=['#ccebc5','violet'])
        plt.ylabel('Percentage Status %')

In this group of crosstab plots we can see that convert more:

  • Leads that are professionals
  • Leads which their first_interaction through the website
  • Leads with high or medium level of completion of their profiles online have better probabilities of being converted to paid customers. This variables are analyzed separately one from the other, so these are not final conclussions. The rest of the variables seem not to have a clear effect in the convertion chances.

Categorical variables with counts per Status

In [118]:
# Function to plot stacked bar plots

def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
    """
    Print the category counts and plot a stacked bar chart

    data: dataframe
    predictor: independent variable
    target: target variable
    """
    count = data[predictor].nunique()
    sorter = data[target].value_counts().index[-1]
    tab1 = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], margins = True).sort_values(
        by = sorter, ascending = False
    )
    print(tab1)
    print("-" * 120)
    tab = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], normalize = "index").sort_values(
        by = sorter, ascending = False
    )
    pastel_colors = ['#ccebc5','violet']
    tab.plot(kind = "bar", stacked = True, figsize = (count + 1, 5),
             color=['#ccebc5','violet'])
    plt.legend(
        loc = "lower left",
        frameon = False,
    )
    plt.legend(loc = "upper left", bbox_to_anchor = (1, 1))
    plt.show()
In [119]:
# Visualize several Stacked Barplots for categorical variables with the real counts per STATUS to
#validate conclussions observed in the previous graphs
stacked_barplot(data, "current_occupation", "status")

status                 0     1   All
current_occupation                  
All                 3223  1375  4598
Professional        1681   927  2608
Unemployed          1053   383  1436
Student              489    65   554
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity 927 (67%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), are professionals. Then 383 are unemployed and just 65 are students. In quantity the leads that are professional (2,608) are way higher than the unemployed (1,436) and the students (554), therefore professionals would have more chances to convert since the leads are more in quantity.

In [120]:
stacked_barplot(data, "last_activity", "status")
color=['#ccebc5','violet']

status               0     1   All
last_activity                     
All               3223  1375  4598
Email Activity    1579   690  2269
Website Activity   677   423  1100
Phone Activity     967   262  1229
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Within the internal analysis of last_activity the website activity have better convertion rate with almost 39%

  • Email Activity: 690/2269 = 30.4%
  • Website Activity:423/1100 = 38.5%
  • Phone Activity: 262/1229 = 21.3%

But in absolute terms the last_activity that predict more paid leads is Email Activity. I would think that this is because the lead is already in the last phase, right before the convertion to paid lead, or in fact is already enrolled.

  • Email Activity: 690
  • Website Activity: 423
  • Phone Activity: 262
In [121]:
stacked_barplot(data, "first_interaction", "status")
color=['#ccebc5','violet']

status                0     1   All
first_interaction                  
All                3223  1375  4598
Website            1379  1157  2536
Mobile App         1844   218  2062
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity 1,157 (84%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), had their first_interaction by website, the rest (218) aprox. 16% had their fisrt_interaction by the mobile app. When we just analyze the category, the website has 1,157/ 2,536 positive cases (46%), in comparison with the mobile_app that just a little 218/2,062 positives (~11%). In both scenarios, in absolute terms and by category, the website first interaction has more changes to convert to a paid customer.

In [122]:
stacked_barplot(data, "profile_completed", "status")
color=['#ccebc5','violet']

status                0     1   All
profile_completed                  
All                3223  1375  4598
High               1311   944  2255
Medium             1813   423  2236
Low                  99     8   107
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity 944 (~69%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), have high level of their profiled completed. This variable has good chances to predict the convertion to paid customers, the quantity of leads are high in total, high level has 2255 leads and medium level has 2236 leads, with a total between them of 4491 out of the 4598 ~ 98% profile_completed, and the internal convertion rate is high as well per category, having high profile completion 944/255 ~ 42% and medium profile completion 423/2236 ~ 19%, which is not despreciable.

In [123]:

status                0     1   All
print_media_type1

No                 2886  1216  4102
Yes                 337   159   496
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity 159 (~11%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), came from newspaper adds. This variable has very small chances to convert to a paid customers the leads are very few in total 496 out of 4598 ~ 11%, and the internal convertion rate is a little bit low as well 159/496 ~ 32%.

In quantity 75 (~5%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), came from magazine adds. This variable has very small chances to convert to a paid customers, the leads are very few in total 233 out of 4598 ~ 5%, and the internal convertion rate is a little bit low as well 75/233 ~32%.

In [125]:
stacked_barplot(data, "digital_media", "status")
color=['#ccebc5','violet']

status            0     1   All
digital_media                  
All            3223  1375  4598
No             2864  1207  4071
Yes             359   168   527
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity 168 (~12%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), came from digital_media. This variable has very small chances to convert to a paid customers, the leads are very few in total 527 out of 4598 ~ 11%, and the internal convertion rate is a little bit low as well 168/527 ~ 32%.

In [126]:
stacked_barplot(data, "educational_channels", "status")
color=['#ccebc5','violet']

status                   0     1   All
educational_channels                  
All                   3223  1375  4598
No                    2716  1178  3894
Yes                    507   197   704
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity 197 (~14%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), came from educational_channels. This variable has better chances to convert to a paid customers than the previous ones, the leads that come from this variable are in total 704 out of 4598 ~ 15%, but the internal convertion rate is a little bit lower than the previous variables with 197/704 ~ 28%.

In [127]:
stacked_barplot(data, "referral", "status")
color=['#ccebc5','violet']

status       0     1   All
referral                  
All       3223  1375  4598
No        3193  1312  4505
Yes         30    63    93
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In quantity only 63 (4%) of the total of leads that became paid customers (1,375), came from referrals. Eventhough, 63 out of the 93 total that were referred, is a good convertion rate (~68%). This should be highlighted as a marketing campaign to increase the quantity of referrals since this variable has a high chance of convertion

***Observations for Crosstab plots**: The following observations and responses are extracted from the previous set of crosstab plots

  • Response to question 1: The current occupation affect lead status. It shows that more than 35% of the professionals and more than 25% of the leads that are unemployed decided to participate in the program. Very little portion, on the other hand, less than 10% of the group that are already studying, decide to join the program. Professionals may want to be always with updated skills or are enrolled by their employees, and the unemployed try to reskill to get better job opportunities.

  • Response to question 2: Yes, the company first impression on the customer have definetely an impact. More than 40% of the leads that interact through the website join the program, but just a ~10% of the lead that interact via mobile app convert to paid customers. Maybe the website provides a better view of the available programs and easier interaction than the mobile app.

  • Response to question 3: This is an interesting conclussion, even when in a previous graph we saw the the email way of interaction is the most common in absolute terms, the website interaction with ~38% is the one that work best to convert to paid customers more than the email and the phone interactions. The phone interaction has the smaller chances to convert the leads to paid customers.

  • Response to question 4: The channel with the highest lead convertion rate ~68% is referral in terms of effectiveness since none of the other channels like print media, digital media, educational channels have better convertion rates even when they have higher quantity of leads. If we just see the row numbers the channel with the highest quantity of leads that became paid customer is educational with 197, followed by digital_media with 168 leads, but in the end, all of the channels represent very small portions to blindly be considered as important variables for the convertion. Even when we have very few leads per channel, given the fact that there are always multiple sources involved, each of channels have an approximate of 5-10% as portion of the whole marketing source of leads, and it is interesting that internally the convertion per category goes around the 32%, which means that once the lead is in the pipeline, depending of the ability and tactics utilized, the leads have a fair chance to become paid customers.

  • Response to question 5: Having more details about the prospect definitely increase the chances of convertion to paid customer. The least we know about the lead, the least oppotunities we have to convert. High level of profile completion represent 69% of the total of paid customers. As I previously explained before, this variable has good chances to predict the convertion to paid customers, since there are 2 positive factors, the quantity of leads that have completed their profiles in high/medium level are high in total, 4491 out of the 4598 ~ 98%, and the internal convertion rate is high as well per category for the high profile completion 944/255 ~ 42%.

Data Preprocessing¶

  • Missing value treatment was not neccesary
  • Feature engineering
  • Outlier detection and treatment: Decision trees are good at handling outlier, so I am not going to treat for outliers
  • Preparing data for modeling
  • Any other preprocessing steps

Feature engineering

In [128]:
#Create new column
data['avg_time_per_visit'] = data['time_spent_on_website']/data['website_visits']

#Verify the last column created avg_time_per_visit at the end

In [131]:
#Plot Wrt Target to visualize new variable
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "avg_time_per_visit", "status")

If we do not take the outliers in consideration, in general we can see that the leads that spent more than 300 seconds/5 minutes in the website in average, are the leads that tend to convert and enroll in the program. This new specific factor have lots of sense since the leads that are really interested are going to spend more time in researching about details of the programs.

In [132]:
# Binning the age column to get a range of age instead of specific numerical age
data['age'] = original_data['age'].copy() # Reset 'age' to original numerical values
bins = [18, 30, 40, 50, 60, np.inf]
labels = ['18-30', '31-40', '41-50', '51-60', '61+']
data['age'] = pd.cut(data['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

Preparing data for modeling

In [133]:
# ONE HOT ENCODE/Creating dummy variables for the categorical columns
# drop_first=True is used to avoid redundant variables
data = pd.get_dummies(
    data,
    columns = data.select_dtypes(include = ["object", "category"]).columns.tolist(),
    drop_first = True,
)

#Visualize the new dummy columns created
data.head()
Out[134]:
website_visits time_spent_on_website page_views_per_visit status avg_time_per_visit age_31-40 age_41-50 age_51-60 age_61+ current_occupation_Student current_occupation_Unemployed first_interaction_Website profile_completed_Low profile_completed_Medium last_activity_Phone Activity last_activity_Website Activity print_media_type1_Yes print_media_type2_Yes digital_media_Yes educational_channels_Yes referral_Yes
0 7 1639 1.861 1 234.142857 False False True False False True True False False False True True False True False False
