4 4 600 16.914 0 150.000000 False False False False True False True False False False False False False False False False

The number of columns has increased after the creation of the dummy variables. For each categorical column the first category has been removed to be the reference variable.

In [135]:
# Separating independent variables and the target variable status
x = data.drop('status',axis=1)
y = data['status']

# Ensure  that no NaNs or Infs in x before splitting
x.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True)
x.fillna(0, inplace=True)

# Print NaNs or Infs Infinites if present in x
print("Number of NaN values in x before train-test split:", x.isnull().sum().sum())
print("Number of Inf values in x before train-test split:", np.isinf(x.select_dtypes(include=np.number)).sum().sum())

Number of NaN values in x before train-test split: 0
Number of Inf values in x before train-test split: 0
In [136]:
# Splitting the dataset into train and test datasets
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size = 0.3, shuffle = True, random_state = 1)
In [137]:
# Checking the shape of the train and test data
print("Shape of Training set : ", x_train.shape)
print("Shape of test set : ", x_test.shape)

Shape of Training set :  (3218, 20)
Shape of test set :  (1380, 20)
In [138]:
# Importing again classification metrics to measure the performance for the models
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score

# Function to compute different metrics to check performance of this classification model
def model_performance_classification(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance
    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    pred = model.predict(predictors)                  # Predict using the independent variables
    prob = model.predict_proba(predictors)[:, 1]      # Get probabilities for ROC AUC

    accuracy = accuracy_score(target, pred)           # To compute Accuracy
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)         # To compute Precision
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)               # To compute Recall
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)                       # To compute F1-score
    roc_auc = roc_auc_score(target, prob)             # To compute ROC AUC

    # Creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "Accuracy": accuracy,
            "Precision": precision,
            "Recall": recall,
            "F1-score": f1,
            "ROC AUC": roc_auc,
        },
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf

Building a Decision Tree model¶

Decision Tree Classifier - Train and Test

In [139]:
# Decision Tree Classifier
dt_classifier = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state = 1)

# Fitting the model (ensuring it's fitted on the most recent x_train)
dt_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the training data
dt_classifier_perf_train = model_performance_classification(dt_classifier, x_train, y_train)
dt_classifier_perf_train
Out[139]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.998757 1.0 0.995897 0.997945 0.999996

First run: For the train performance my accurancy 0.99, precision 1, recall 0.99, F1 0.99 and ROC AUC 0.99, given the fact that all my scores are very high, it seems my train data is memorized by the model and it will be difficult to work well in the test data. We will see.

In [140]:
# DT Model Performance on the test data
dt_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(dt_classifier, x_test, y_test)
dt_classifier_perf_test
Out[140]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.797101 0.638889 0.69 0.663462 0.770347

First run: Exactly as I thought, for my test performance my accurancy 0.79, precision 0.64 , recall 0.69, F1 0.66 and ROC AUC 0.77 are low, very far from my scores in the trainning data. My model is overfitted and it is not capturing the real structure of the problem. Maybe because I did not correct outliers it is capturing noise. I will have to do something to get a better model.

In [141]:
# Limit the decision tree's depth so that we can visualize it better
from sklearn import tree
features = list(x.columns)

# Building the model with max_depth=3 and max_depth=4 to decide which is better
dt_classifier_visualize = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state = 1, max_depth=3)

# Fitting the model
dt_classifier_visualize.fit(x_train, y_train)


plt.figure(figsize = (18, 20))
tree.plot_tree(dt_classifier_visualize, feature_names = features, filled = True, fontsize = 12,
               node_ids = True, class_names = ['Not Converted', 'Converted'])
plt.show()

I tried to plot with max depth 4 and the decision tree gets more difficult to read, and gives other few variables than with the depth 3, but I decided to go back and let the graph with depth 3. The white and blue rectangules converted. The rest oranges do not convert.

In [142]:
# Printing the Decision Tree content
print(tree.export_text(dt_classifier_visualize, feature_names=x_train.columns.tolist(), show_weights=True, class_names = ['Not Converted', 'Converted']))

|--- first_interaction_Website <= 0.50
|   |--- time_spent_on_website <= 419.50
|   |   |--- current_occupation_Student <= 0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [719.00, 0.00] class: Not Converted
|   |   |--- current_occupation_Student >  0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [102.00, 3.00] class: Not Converted
|   |--- time_spent_on_website >  419.50
|   |   |--- last_activity_Website Activity <= 0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [383.00, 78.00] class: Not Converted
|   |   |--- last_activity_Website Activity >  0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [95.00, 57.00] class: Not Converted
|--- first_interaction_Website >  0.50
|   |--- time_spent_on_website <= 413.50
|   |   |--- profile_completed_Medium <= 0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [242.00, 244.00] class: Converted
|   |   |--- profile_completed_Medium >  0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [463.00, 9.00] class: Not Converted
|   |--- time_spent_on_website >  413.50
|   |   |--- last_activity_Phone Activity <= 0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [115.00, 497.00] class: Converted
|   |   |--- last_activity_Phone Activity >  0.50
|   |   |   |--- weights: [124.00, 87.00] class: Not Converted

Observations:

  • Root Node: First_interaction_website <= 0.5. This is the starting point of the decision tree, which means that the First_interaction_website results in the highest information gain among all the features. If the value is less than or equal to 0.5, the left branch is taken, and if it is greater than 0.5, the right branch is taken.

  • Internal Nodes:

    • Time_spent_website <= 419.5
    • Time_spent_website <= 413.5
    • Current Ocupation/Student <= 0.5
    • Last_activity/Website <= 0.5
    • Profile_completed/Medium <= 0.5
    • Last_activity/Phone <= 0.5

Interpretation and Conclusions:

The decision tree starts with the variable first_interaction_Website > 0.50, with a split on the Time_spent_website feature.

  • If the lead first_interaction_Website > 0.50, and then the time_spent_on_website <= 413.50, and the profile_completed_Medium <= 0.50, the lead convert to paid customer = Total samples 486

  • The second path if the lead first_interaction_Website > 0.50, and then the time_spent_on_website > 413.50 and the last_activity_Phone Activity <= 0.50, the lead convert to paid customer = Total samples 612

Note: My tree is truncated and not shown completely to get proper visualization.

Building and evaluating other models¶

Bagging Classifier

In [143]:
# Bagging Classifier
bagging_estimator = BaggingClassifier(random_state = 1)

# Fitting the model
bagging_estimator.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the train data
bagging_estimator_perf_train = model_performance_classification(bagging_estimator, x_train, y_train)
bagging_estimator_perf_train
Out[143]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.987881 0.988518 0.971282 0.979824 0.999109

First run: It happened the same, my train metrics are very high in general above 0.97.

In [144]:
# Model Performance on the test data
bagging_estimator_perf_test = model_performance_classification(bagging_estimator, x_test, y_test)
bagging_estimator_perf_test
Out[144]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.839855 0.745205 0.68 0.711111 0.881677

First run: The train metrics are low, the lowest is the recall with 0.68. Very distance from the train metrics, so overfitted.

Random Forest Classifier

In [145]:
# Random Forest Classifier is the model selected after running several times
rf_classifier = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators = 100, random_state = 1)

# Fitting the model
rf_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the train data
rf_classifier_perf_train = model_performance_classification(rf_classifier, x_train, y_train)
rf_classifier_perf_train
Out[145]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.998757 0.997949 0.997949 0.997949 0.999996

First run: I see the same pattern for this model as well, the train metrics are very very high, which is suspicious.

In [146]:
# Model Performance on the test data
rf_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(rf_classifier, x_test, y_test)
rf_classifier_perf_test
Out[146]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.843478 0.747312 0.695 0.720207 0.902856

First run: For the test performance my accurancy 0.84, precision 0.74, recall 0.69, F1 0.72 and ROC AUC 0.90 are a far in general from my RF train test performance. My RF model is overfitted as well and this problem needs to be corrected since my precision and recall are very distance from train and test, and specially if I am interested in reducing the false negative / RECALL rate, to avoid Extralearn loose potencial paid customers. This problem might be because my data is unbalance the class 0 is bigger than the class 1. I need to level it.

Exercise RF CLASSIFIER 2: Adjusting manually the parameters for the Random Forest and Leveling data weight¶

In [147]:
# RF Model Performance on the train data limiting the complexity of the model
# adjusting number of estimators, balancing the classes, limiting max depth
# increasing min_sample_leaf,
rf_classifier2 = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=300, max_depth=12,
                                        min_samples_split=50,
                                        min_samples_leaf=10,
                                        class_weight='balanced',
                                        max_features='sqrt',
                                        random_state=42)

# Fitting the model RF2
rf_classifier2.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance 2 on the train data
rf_classifier_perf_train2 = model_performance_classification(rf_classifier2, x_train, y_train)
rf_classifier_perf_train2
Out[147]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.864201 0.723794 0.892308 0.799265 0.949002

First run: The firt time I adjusted the RF, I just did the data weight balance, but it was not enough, since the all the train metrics remained very high from 0.99 to 1.

Second run: After adjusting n_estimators=300, max_depth=12, min_samples_split=50, min_samples_leaf=10, class_weight='balanced', max_features='sqrt', random_state=42, I got better precision 0.72, recall 0.89 and F1 0.79 performance metrics for the train dataset.

In [148]:
# Model Performance on the test data after leveling classes
rf_classifier_perf_test2 = model_performance_classification(rf_classifier2, x_test, y_test)
rf_classifier_perf_test2
Out[148]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.823913 0.654832 0.83 0.732084 0.90637

First run: By just balancing classes, I could not improve that much, the test metrics remain very low and far from the train. Recall = 0.69 is the lowest, but precision and F1 are around the 0.72.

Second run: After adjusted several parameters mentioned in the train section, the model was able to improve getting better test performance metrics, more near to the precision, recall and F1 score from the train. Recall improved significantly in test from the first run 0.69, to 0.83 in this second run helping to reduce the false negatives, but Precision, as the trade-off work, decreased from 0.72 to 0.65 which means now my false positive would increase. Anyways this second run after adjusting parameters, is way better than the first run since we were able to overcome the overfitting.

ADA Boost Classifier

In [149]:
# AdaBoost Classifier
ada_classifier = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

# Fitting the model
ada_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the train data
ada_classifier_perf_train = model_performance_classification(ada_classifier, x_train, y_train)
ada_classifier_perf_train
Out[149]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.831572 0.769614 0.633846 0.695163 0.903906

First run: Accuracy 0.83, precision 0.76, Recall 0.63, F1 0.69 and ROC AUC 0.90. ADA classifier gave better metrics in comparison with the previous models, but low still to be acceptable for the case purposes.

In [150]:
# Model Performance on the test data
ada_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(ada_classifier, x_test, y_test)
ada_classifier_perf_test
Out[150]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.807971 0.703927 0.5825 0.637483 0.869128

First run: Accuracy 0,80, precision 0,70, Recall 0.58, F1 0.63 and ROC AUC 0.87. Recall is too low, very far to be a good model to predict paid customers. At least this model is not overfitted.

Gradient Boosting Classifier

In [151]:
# Gradient Boosting Classifier
grad_classifier = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

# Fitting the model
grad_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the train data
grad_classifier_perf_train = model_performance_classification(grad_classifier, x_train, y_train)
grad_classifier_perf_train
Out[151]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.887508 0.834242 0.784615 0.808668 0.950634

First run: Accuracy 0.88, precision 0.83, Recall 0.78, F1 0.80 and ROC AUC 0.95. This results are the best until the moment for the train set, better than in the previous models because are not near to 1 memorizing the train set and overfiting in the end, like in the DT and RF fisrt runs.

In [152]:
# Model Performance on the test data
grad_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(grad_classifier, x_test, y_test)
grad_classifier_perf_test
Out[152]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.844203 0.74026 0.7125 0.726115 0.908446

First run: Accuracy 0,84, precision 0,74, Recall 0.71, F1 0.73 and ROC AUC 0.90. Better recall than in the previous models, not that low, and when compared with the train metrics recall 0.78 is not that far. Not overfitted model

XGBoost Classifier

In [153]:
# Installing the xgboost library using the 'pip' command
# !pip install xgboost
In [154]:
# Importing XGBoost Classifier
from xgboost import XGBClassifier

# XGBoost Classifier with added use_label_encoder and eval_metric for modern XGBoost versions
xgb = XGBClassifier(random_state = 1, use_label_encoder=False, eval_metric='logloss')

# Fitting the model
xgb.fit(x_train,y_train)

# Model Performance on the train data
xgb_perf_train = model_performance_classification(xgb, x_train, y_train)
xgb_perf_train
Out[154]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.997203 0.991853 0.998974 0.995401 0.999778

First run: Accuracy 0.99, precision 0.99, Recall 0.99, F1 0.99 and ROC AUC 0.99. All very high, following the same pattern than previous models DT and RF.

In [155]:
# Model Performance on the test data
xgb_perf_test = model_performance_classification(xgb, x_test, y_test)
xgb_perf_test
Out[155]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.830435 0.715026 0.69 0.70229 0.898661

First run: Accuracy 0,83, precision 0,71, Recall 0.69, F1 0.70 and ROC AUC 0.89.

Model Performance evaluation and improvement¶

In [156]:
models_test_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        dt_classifier_perf_test.T,
        bagging_estimator_perf_test.T,
        rf_classifier_perf_test.T,
        rf_classifier_perf_test2.T,
        ada_classifier_perf_test.T,
        grad_classifier_perf_test.T,
        xgb_perf_test.T
    ],
    axis = 1,
)

models_test_comp_df.columns = [
    "Decision Tree Classifier",
    "Bagging Classifier",
    "Random Forest Classifier",
    "Random Forest Classifier 2",
    "Ada Boost Classifier",
    "Gradient Boosting Classifier",
    "XG Boost Classifier"]

print("Test performance comparison:")

models_test_comp_df.T

Test performance comparison:
Out[156]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
Decision Tree Classifier 0.797101 0.638889 0.6900 0.663462 0.770347
Bagging Classifier 0.839855 0.745205 0.6800 0.711111 0.881677
Random Forest Classifier 0.843478 0.747312 0.6950 0.720207 0.902856
Random Forest Classifier 2 0.823913 0.654832 0.8300 0.732084 0.906370
Ada Boost Classifier 0.807971 0.703927 0.5825 0.637483 0.869128
Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.844203 0.740260 0.7125 0.726115 0.908446
XG Boost Classifier 0.830435 0.715026 0.6900 0.702290 0.898661

First run: Between all the models that I run, the Gradient Boosting Classifier worked better. The test recall was 0.71 for the first run, with the test sample 0.30 and without leveling the data.

Second run: ***After I adjusted parameters for the Random Forest Classifier manually following best practices to reduce the complexity of the model in order to avoid the overfitting, I got Random Forest Classifier 2 with Recall 0.83 primarily focusing in that missing a positive customer is very costly. Anyways as we do not know the cost involved in this business, I prefer to mantain the F1 as the metric to take decision of which is the best model, and for this case, before tuning with Gridsearch, it is the Random Forest Classifier 2, and in second place the Gradient Boosting model with recall 0.71**.

As requested in the rubric, I am going to follow the steps to tune the Decision Tree, again tune the Random Forest Classifier and the Gradient Boosting Classifier that was my first best model, and comment about the results after the model comparison table.

Tuning Decision Tree, Random Forest and Gradient Boosting Classifier¶

Tuned Decision Tree¶

In [157]:
#GRID SEARCH METHOD FOR TUNING DT
dt_tuned = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state = 1)

# Grid of parameters to choose from and entropy as recommended split criteria
dt_parameters = {
    "max_depth": [5, 7, None],
    "min_samples_leaf": [1, 5, 10],
    "criterion": ["gini", "entropy"]
}

# Run the grid search with f1 to balance between recall and precision
dt_grid_obj = GridSearchCV(dt_tuned, dt_parameters, scoring = 'f1', cv = 5)

# Ensure x_train and y_train are defined by running previous cells
dt_grid_obj = dt_grid_obj.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Set the rf_tuned_classifier to the best combination of parameters
dt_tuned_classifier = dt_grid_obj.best_estimator_

dt_tuned_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the test data
dt_tuned_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(dt_tuned_classifier, x_test, y_test)

dt_tuned_classifier_perf_test
Out[157]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.837681 0.714634 0.7325 0.723457 0.88714

The model DT improved a little bit my recall after the tuning the DT.

Tuned Random Forest Classifier¶

In [158]:
#GRID SEARCH METHOD FOR TUNING RF
rf_tuned = RandomForestClassifier(random_state = 1)

# Grid of parameters to choose from
rf_parameters = {"n_estimators": [100, 110, 120],

    "max_depth": [5, 7, None],

    "max_features": [0.8, 1]
             }

# Run the grid search with scoring f1 to balance
rf_grid_obj = GridSearchCV(rf_tuned, rf_parameters, scoring = 'f1', cv = 5)

rf_grid_obj = rf_grid_obj.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Set the rf_tuned_classifier to the best combination of parameters
rf_tuned_classifier = rf_grid_obj.best_estimator_

rf_tuned_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the test data
rf_tuned_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(rf_tuned_classifier, x_test, y_test)

rf_tuned_classifier_perf_test
Out[158]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.853623 0.761905 0.72 0.74036 0.9132

The recall is now 0.72 and precision 0.76 both of them have improved, and the F1 is 0.74, similar to the previous exercise when I tuned manually the Random Forest Classifier 2.

In [159]:
#Using RandomizedSearchCV for this RFC


from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV
from scipy.stats import randint

## Define the RandomForestClassifier
rf_tuned = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)

# # Define the parameter distributions for randomized search
rf_parameters = {
     "n_estimators": randint(100, 121),  # Adjust the range as needed
     "max_depth": [5, 7, None],
     "max_features": [0.8, 1]
 }

# # Run the randomized search
rf_random_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
     rf_tuned,
     param_distributions=rf_parameters,
     n_iter=10,  # Adjust the number of iterations as needed
     scoring='f1',
     cv=5,
     random_state=1,
     n_jobs=-1
 )

rf_random_search.fit(x_train, y_train)

# # Get the best estimator
rf_tuned_classifier = rf_random_search.best_estimator_

# # Fit the model with the best estimator
rf_tuned_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# # Model performance on the test data
rf_tuned_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(rf_tuned_classifier, x_test, y_test)
rf_tuned_classifier_perf_test
Out[159]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.853623 0.761905 0.72 0.74036 0.91326

After running both tuning methods I got the same results. I will take the fisrt method to compile the resuts.

Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier¶

In [160]:
#GRID SEARCH METHOD FOR TUNING GC
gb_tuned = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state = 1)

# Grid of parameters to choose from
gb_parameters = {"n_estimators": [100, 110, 120],

    "max_depth": [5, 7, None],

    "max_features": [0.8, 1]
             }

# Run the grid search with scoring f1 to balance
gb_grid_obj = GridSearchCV(gb_tuned, gb_parameters, scoring = 'f1', cv = 5)

gb_grid_obj = gb_grid_obj.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Set the gb_tuned_classifier to the best combination of parameters
gb_tuned_classifier = gb_grid_obj.best_estimator_

gb_tuned_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Model Performance on the test data
gb_tuned_classifier_perf_test = model_performance_classification(gb_tuned_classifier, x_test, y_test)
gb_tuned_classifier_perf_test
Out[160]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
0 0.848551 0.741772 0.7325 0.737107 0.907847

The best model was since the beginnig, before manually tuning, Gradient Boosting, and after the tuning with gridsearch, between the Decision Tree and the Tuned Random Forest (RF2) and the Tuned RF with gridsearch, I can see that the Gradient Boosting got better performance metrics than DT and RF/RF2, much more balanced between precision and recall, giving precision a little bit more importance, which means that if the business use this model, would prioritize in focusing in leads that are worthed, reducing the false positives and do not wasting time in bad leads.

Final Model Decision after comparing all the models¶

In [161]:
# Compare the performance of the models in the test set
models_test_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        dt_classifier_perf_test.T,
        dt_tuned_classifier_perf_test.T,
        bagging_estimator_perf_test.T,
        rf_classifier_perf_test.T,
        rf_classifier_perf_test2.T,
        rf_tuned_classifier_perf_test.T,
        ada_classifier_perf_test.T,
        grad_classifier_perf_test.T,
        gb_tuned_classifier_perf_test.T,
        xgb_perf_test.T,
    ],
    axis = 1,
)

models_test_comp_df.columns = [
    "Decision Tree Classifier",
    "TUNED Decision Tree Classifier",
    "Bagging Classifier",
    "Random Forest Classifier",
    "TUNED Manually Random Forest Classifier 2",
    "TUNED Random Forest Classifier",
    "Ada Boost Classifier",
    "Gradient Boosting Classifier",
    "TUNED Gradient Boosting Classifier",
    "XG Boost Classifier"]

print("Test performance comparison the includes 4 tuned models:")

models_test_comp_df.T

Test performance comparison the includes 4 tuned models:
Out[161]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1-score ROC AUC
Decision Tree Classifier 0.797101 0.638889 0.6900 0.663462 0.770347
TUNED Decision Tree Classifier 0.837681 0.714634 0.7325 0.723457 0.887140
Bagging Classifier 0.839855 0.745205 0.6800 0.711111 0.881677
Random Forest Classifier 0.843478 0.747312 0.6950 0.720207 0.902856
TUNED Manually Random Forest Classifier 2 0.823913 0.654832 0.8300 0.732084 0.906370
TUNED Random Forest Classifier 0.853623 0.761905 0.7200 0.740360 0.913260
Ada Boost Classifier 0.807971 0.703927 0.5825 0.637483 0.869128
Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.844203 0.740260 0.7125 0.726115 0.908446
TUNED Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.848551 0.741772 0.7325 0.737107 0.907847
XG Boost Classifier 0.830435 0.715026 0.6900 0.702290 0.898661

Observations:

  • Accuracy: For this case is not that relevant, so even when my rates are high, on or above 0.80 for all the models tested, I need to evaluate precision and recall as I have mentioned before to avoid loosing portencial customer or suggest the company to loose resources in leads that are not going to convert.

  • Precision and Recall during previous steps I realized and commented that the performance metrics tell me that my model was not predicting well after the comparison between train and test metrics per each model applied. In the first run I would say it was totally overffited.

  • For the Decision Tree I improved the recall from 0,69 to 0.73 a decent amount after the tuning

  • For Randon Forest during the first run the recall was 1 for train and 0.69 for test, overfitted as well. After tunning manually and with gridsearch, the model improved the metrics improving recall and decreasing precision in the RF2, and improving both of the metrics after using gridsearch with score f1. Between the 3 Random Forest lines we can see the trade-off between precision and recall and at the end a TUNED more balanced model.

  • GRadient Boosting is my best balance model after tuning. 0.74 preciosion. 0.73 recall and 0.73 f1. This model was not overfitted since the first run as I explained below the train metrics previously.

  • F1 score: The best F1 metrics are in the Tuned Random Forest 0.740 and Tuned Gradient Boosting models 0.737. By definition,this metric is the harmonic mean between precision and recall and could be useful if we just want to see the model performance under one single metric, in this case it helps to balance the false negatives, when my best model predict that a lead will not converted, but in real the lead get converted, and balance as well the false positives when the model predict that a lead will get converted, but in the end, the lead does not convert. Thinking that the data set is imbalance for the first run, this metric is useful for the customer prediction analysis and it is my preference variable.

  • ROC AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under the Curve) is 0.91 which is near 1, theoretically my model is good enough to distinguish between the leads that will convert vs. the leads that will not convert to paid customers. BUT, this metric is more applied for general comparison and does not tell us how the model performs at the threshold we will actually use. The model could have high AUC, as in all of these cases, but still have poor recall or precision, and that is not what we need for this specific case when we try to achieve conversion of the leads. So this metric will not be considered as a metric for the final comparison of the best models.

Feature Importance for the Tuned Models DT, RF and GB¶

These are going to be the features that contribute the most to the model's predictions. We will see which are the features that the 3 models have in common and get the conclusions for the profile of leads. I will comment below.

In [162]:
# Plotting the feature importance for the best model (dt_tuned_classifier)
features = list(x.columns)
importances = dt_tuned_classifier.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize = (8, 6))
plt.title('Feature Importances for Tuned Decision Tree Classifier')
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color = '#ccebc5', align = 'center')
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [features[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

For the DT tuned classifier the most important variables for the prediction are:

  • Time spent on webiste
  • First interaction / Website
  • Profile completed / Medium
  • Current Occupation / Student The fith variable is Current Occupation/Unemployed
In [163]:
# Plotting the feature importance for the model (rf_tuned_classifier)
features = list(x.columns)
importances = rf_tuned_classifier.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize = (8, 6))
plt.title('Feature Importances for Tuned Random Forest Classifier')
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color = '#E0BBE4', align = 'center')
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [features[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

For the RF tuned classifier the most important variables for the prediction are the same that in the DT tuned, but the first 2 variables inverted in order:

  • First interaction / Website
  • Time spent on webiste
  • Profile completed / Medium
  • Current Occupation / Student The fith variable is Last Activity/Phone
In [164]:
# Plotting the feature importance for the model (gb_tuned_classifier)
features = list(x.columns)
importances = gb_tuned_classifier.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize = (8, 6))
plt.title('Feature Importances for Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier')
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color = '#FFF9C4', align = 'center')
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [features[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

For the GB tuned classifier the most important variables for the prediction are the same to the Decicion Tree tuned model, and in the same order:

  • First interaction / Website
  • Time spent on webiste
  • Profile completed / Medium
  • Current Occupation / Student

Curiously in this model the 5th variable is the created variable, average time per visit.

Profile of leads

A Profile of leads which are likely to convert contains the variables with the strongest predictive power, I would focus on the first 3 in the graphs above, taking in consideration that the size of the bars gets reduce from the fourth to the end:

  • The time_spent_on_website and first_interaction through the website are the strongest variables of the model to predict the convertion. We can tell that the user experience on the website can be more informative, engaging, or user-friendly for leads than the mobile app, leading to a higher likelihood of conversion. The website might offer a more comprehensive overview of programs, testimonials, pricing, chat with me option, call me button that works well and facilitates fast phone contact, download the programs once the lead register some personal information, and features that are working well in helping leads make a more informed decision, accessible contact, and finally encouraging the further advance towards the enrollment process. The ***time that a lead spent in the website** looking around, consuming the information and using the features reflect the level of interest in joining a program. All of the features I mentioned are assumptions, if those features are not present yet, these could be a point of improvement for the website, but given the fact that the website interaction works well, I am assuming the website is strong in presence, content and features.

  • The profile_completed_Medium tells about a considerable level of interest and level of engagement from the lead, reflected in the information they share in their profile, and it is a strong indicator of their conversion potential. Nevertheless the decision tree had this variable minus 0.5, which means that when the profile_medium is 0 is going to convert, so it will match with high which had better convertion rate, or even low profile completion, this last one was not good to convert. In general the level of completion would show that the lead is willing to invest time and share its personal information to be contacted and get more information, revealing strong interest which is a crucial step towards a enrollment decision.

There are other variables that appear in the models I would like to comment about:

  • Surpringsingly, **the current_ocupation variable appear as the 4th important feature for the profile. **It happens the same situation with minus 0.5 it means that when the ocupation is not student it will convert more. Student ocupation variable is present in the 3 models and Unemployed ocupation is in 2 of the models. I would think that in general the ocupation has a moderate impact on the likelihood of lead becoming a paying customer. It could be that leads unemployed try to dedicate their free time in upskilling or reskilling, and at the same time pave the way to land a new job position. About the students category, as they have very low level of convertion in the dataset, we would need to recommend to focus specific or limited resources in capturing students to follow their next academic path in Extralearn and verify in practice if the younger profiles convert, but adjusting some business rules to accompany this new strategy as a pilot project, since with the data given, stuadents do not convert to paid customers.

  • Regarding the last_interaction by phone call, it is moderately relevant since appears in 2 models as part of the best 4 or 5 features. As we see in the bivariate analysis, leads that talk by phone are very little in comparison with the email interaction for example. I would assume that leads that are interested, look for help call the support center service. The call center have to take advantage of this interested leads and follow the steps to capture the lead and enroll it. I would revise this option since maybe the call center has not being doing a good job because the interaction by phone is reduced, and the email interaction is a stronger indicator to convert.

  • Contrary to what I thought at the beginning about the referral variable, here we see that in real, after the whole analysis is performed, the referral variable has very little influence on the conversion in this model at the present. I attribute this lack of power to the small quantity of leads that come from referrals. Maybe increasing the leads that come from this category, Extralearn can increase the convertion based on this variable and become in the future one of the profile variables.

  • The variables related to the sourcing/origin of the lead through printed media like print_media_type1, print_media_type2, educational_channel and digital_media have no real influence on the conversion in the model. This suggests that, once a lead arrives on the platform, the channel through which they initially arrived has less weight in their final conversion decision than their behavior, interaction and engagement that the leads can get from the features website provides.

Top Model Performance Comparison¶

We are going to visualize the key performance metrics of our best models to get a clearer comparison.

In [165]:
# Select top models for comparison. I am going to plot just 3 models
top_models = models_test_comp_df.T.sort_values(by='F1-score', ascending=False).head(3)

# Metrics to plot just 3 more important for the case
metrics_to_plot = ['Precision', 'Recall', 'F1-score']

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
sns.barplot(data=top_models[metrics_to_plot].reset_index().melt(id_vars='index'),
            x='index', y='value', hue='variable', palette=['#E0BBE4', '#ccebc5', '#FFF9C4'])
plt.title('Comparison of Top Classification Model Performance')
plt.ylabel('Score')
plt.xlabel('Model')
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.legend(title='Metric')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations:

  • Based on the results obtained after comparing all of the tuned models, Gradient Booting Clasifier is the best-performing model, and as we can see in the graph above is the more balanced model between precision and recall, which is good if we do not know the real business costs and priorities/strategy for the period, or like in this case of study, where the data is very limited in terms of enrollment fees or marketing cost per lead per channel.

  • ***If I would have to choose between the Decision Tree and the Random Forest models, the best model is the Tuned Random Forest**. At the beginning, before tuning, no model was enoughly good, all of them were overfitted as I already explained in the previous steps.

  • Tuning the hyperparameters of the best models with gridsearch was a crucial/important step that helped to optimize the model's performance by finding the best set of hyperparameters that work well for the dataset. The manual tuning that I did for the Randon Forest 2, incrased a lot the recall which could be good as well if the business needs were more focus on only capturing the TP leads, but paying the price of decreasing the precision, not really achieving the balance.

  • Depending of the business needs, any of these 3 models and features is good enough to improve the marketing operations, lead capture and convertion.

In [166]:
# Function to print the classification report

def metrics_score(actual, predicted):
    print(classification_report(actual, predicted))
    con_matrix = confusion_matrix(actual, predicted)
    plt.figure(figsize = (8, 5))
    sns.heatmap(con_matrix, annot = True,  fmt = '.2f', xticklabels = ['Not Converted', 'Converted'],
                yticklabels = ['Not Converted', 'Converted'])
    plt.ylabel('Actual')
    plt.xlabel('Predicted')
    plt.show()

print('Classification Report and Confusion Matrix for Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier:')
metrics_score(y_test, gb_tuned_classifier.predict(x_test))

Classification Report and Confusion Matrix for Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.89      0.90      0.89       980
           1       0.74      0.73      0.74       400

    accuracy                           0.85      1380
   macro avg       0.82      0.81      0.82      1380
weighted avg       0.85      0.85      0.85      1380

***Observations for the Confusion Matrix** The plot shows the actual versus predicted counts:

  • True Negatives (Top-Left): Leads correctly predicted as 'Not Converted'. ***Precision for class 0 (Not Converted): count 878 out of 980, meaning 89% of leads predicted as 'Not Converted' were actually 'Not Converted'**.

  • True Positives (Bottom-Right): Leads correctly predicted as 'Converted'. Recall for class 1 (Converted): count 293 TP out of all the actual positives (400 = 293+107), meaning 73% of actual 'Converted' leads were correctly identified, almost 3 out 4 positive predictions are right, my model is detecting the positives now. This is way better than the 46% that I have gotten initially in my first overfitted model

  • False Negatives (Bottom-Left): Leads incorrectly predicted as 'Not Converted' (actual was 'Converted'). Precision for class 1 (Converted):107, 293 TP out of all the predicted positives (395 = 293+102), meaning 74% of predicted as 'Not Converted' were actually 'Converted'

  • False Positives (Top-Right): Leads incorrectly predicted as 'Converted' (actual was 'Not Converted'). Recall for class 0 (Not Converted): 102, leads predicted as Converted that actual 'Not Converted'.

  • F1-score for class 1 (Converted): 0.74, which is a balanced measure of precision and recall.

  • Accuracy: The overall accuracy of the model is 0.85 (85%).

The model shows good overall performance, with a reasonable balance between precision and recall for the 'Converted' class.

Insigths

  • The Preliminary Data Analysis gave us from the beginning certain clues about the variables that play an important role in the prediction. Even when we apply simple statistical analysis, we discovered the behavior of the leads related to each variable like interaction by website and time_per_visit that later were reassured by the most important features useful for the prediction.

  • The first interaction by website is a good predictor, and it is more related to the nature of the business and the way that people interact at the present to find online content on the web than in any other place. The website usually has a strong presence and is the first face of the company for the leads. Supported by the numbers, the leads that converted to paid customers are 42,66% (1,375/4,598). Out of the 1,375, a quantity of 1,157 (84%) a high percentage had their first interaction by the website.

  • Curiously, the number of website_visits, since the beginning was not a clear factor of the prediction, telling us that the even when a lead visit the website just 2 or 3 times, the most important fact is not the quantity of visits, since it is in average 3 for positive/negative outcomes, but it is the time that the lead invest in its research about the offer/programs.

  • The time_spent_on_website variable was on several ocassions during the analysis targeted as an important predictor. The interest is shown clearly by the time the people invest in reading and researching. This can be clearly seen in the distribution plot wrt target and at the end in the decision tree and random forest model results.

  • The last interaction of the customers, last_activity variable, for me was not a clear predictor since given the dataset that we have, we can not really see in what stage is the lead. The leads are in constant evolution, and in educational field, sometimes the people take long time or at least several months to decide what to do, if enroll or not. At the preliminary analysis having a last_activity variable without any other variable that talks about the stage in the pipeline that the lead is, was pretty difficult that this variable is used as predictor, BUT after running the decision tree and random forest model my preliminary conclusion was not proven. The phone activity might serve as a variable to confirm the paid customer status.

  • For customer‑prediction models, not a single metric is “the most important” in all cases, it is going to depend on what business consequences the companies care more about. The cost of the model mistakes need to be identified to take a final decision on what metric we are going to focus on, BUT I would recommend to consider ***F1 is a good metric to simplify the problem** when getting a real cost per customer lost, or the real cost of an specific campaign per lead was too difficult or time consuming to calculate. Usually the small companies do not have robust cost control systems in place to define the exact cost per business object.

Actionable Business Recommendations

  1. Analyse the income in terms of the profile of leads that more convert. Having already identified the main 3 features of the profile, Extralearn can filter the enrolled leads that match the profile out of the whole population of customers to determine if in fact, this group of customers represent at least more than the 50% of the income. This would be a test for the profile determined previously. If the profile support the income in good proportion it means Extralearn can focus on assuring that those type of leads are well taking care during the lead lifetime, by implementing aditional steps or performance indicators per stage of the funnel. If the current income is not really supported by the profile, which can be another situation with much more reasons involved given the time variations, changes in market or changes in offer, it would be good to determine the best combination of features taking the income variable as part of the dataset, the features of clients that in fact make the business profitable.

  2. In general, for this case, all of the sources of leads, represent by itself a very few portion of the total of the leads that became paid customers within each category. In order to convert more leads to paid customers, first we need to increase the quantity and the quality of leads. I would recommend to the marketing department to diversify even more the sources of leads by:

  • Defining or redefining the strategy in terms of the offer, prices and align the marketing objetives to achieve the strategy taking as well in consideration the income analysis from point 1. The programs available in their offer vs. the type of leads that they are going to focus on to maximize their income, for example with current occupation students. In the data we saw the paid customers were mainly people older than 40 years old and around 55 years old, mostly professionals or unemployed. ExtraLearn could start a pilot project on getting younger leads/students since the problem narrative include upskill, not just reskill.

  • Promoting their programs in educational fairs organized by local governments where the people go specifically looking for programs online.

  • Analyze and implement a good price strategy that allows young or unemployed people to join their programs.

  • Getting databases from specific high schools that are aligned to their new iniciative of getting younger leads/ client target to send focus campaigns for those kids that look for online education in the areas where ExtraLearn has an academic offer.

  • Partnering with recognized education counselors or educational consulting companies to offer an incentive program that articulates academic guidance and support to clients/professionals and unempoyed leads more than 40 yo mainly, or students of all ages pointing out the ExtraLearn programs available online.

  • Sending targeted messages to users, professionals, in Linkedin filtered by specific job titles that match with the ExtraLearn academic offer.

  1. Once the leads are in the pipeline, ExtraLearn needs to improve the capacity to convert, by creating new ways to keep the lead hot, assuring easy access to information, resolving doubts about the programs offered, providing a fast enrollment process and payment flexibility. All the stages are important to keep the lead hooked until it takes the final decision of enrollment, for example:

  • Specific follow up by email and phone, double the efforts, for the leads that spend more than 5 minutes on the website per visit and visit the website more than 3 times which is the average. This is a recommendation made from the combination of some of the previous analysis.

  • Tuition calculators that show real costs after financial aid, simulators can be added to the website to give a fast idea of the investment, useful for the people that do not want to talk until they know how much it will cost.

  • Offering flexibility and different ways to pay depending on the price of the program.

  • Offering free course modules to showcase the value of their programs can be a good option for the difficult leads, the ones that are interested but need a push to take the decision.
